So basically when we're doing statistical testing, let's say we have two models with similar accuracies.
And these models with similar accuracies. Let's say model one is a generative model model two is a discriminative model.
And we say on data set one, we have this result on data set two, we have another result on data set three, we have another result.
And so then the question is how can we tell if the differences are due to consistent trends that will hold on other data sets or if they are kind of random noise due to the fact that we have one.
And so the way we do this is through statistical significance testing.
So I'm going to cover this briefly in this class, but you can see a drawer at all for an overview and also we're going to have a recitation on how to actually run statistical significance.
So you can take a look at that there.
And so the basic idea is given a quantity, we test certain values of uncertainty with respect to the quantity.
So number one is a p value.
And the p value is what is the probability that a difference with another quantity is by chance.
And so a lower p value means more like we had a having a significant difference.
Usually the threshold for saying that we have a significant difference is there's a 5% chance 0.05 that this difference between the models was due to chance or like data sampling or things like that.
So p less than 0.05 is kind of a threshold for significance.
Another thing that we can measure is confidence intervals and the confidence interval is what is the range under which we could expect another trial to fall and I'll talk about both of these.
And this is another concept called paired versus unpaired tests.
And in unpaired test, this means we compare the means of a quantity on two unrelated groups.
